text: I'm Jacques Fontaine, Thoracic Surgeon at Moffitt Cancer Center, and I will be describing our robotic right lower lobectomy technique. This is a patient with a T-N-N-M-O adenocarcinoma of the lung. As can be seen here on CT scan, the patient has a bulky level XI hyaluron lymph node as well as a right lower lobe primary tumor. Preoperative endobronchial ultrasound showed no evidence of mediastinal lymph node involvement in station four and seven. A PET scan showed intense FDG avidity, not only in the primary right lower lobe tumor, but also in the bulky level eleven hyaluron lymph node. No evidence of distant disease. We offer the patient surgical resection followed by adjuvant therapy.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide the infrapulmonary ligaments up to the infrapulmonary vein, with an 8mm, 30-degree camera placed in the eighth intercostal space. Two 12mm ports are positioned on either side in the same intercostal space. Retraction is achieved using a thoracic tip-up and a sponge ball inserted from the fourth intercostal space anteriorly. A lymphadenectomy is performed on level nine and level eight parasophageal lymph nodes after the inferior pulmonary ligament is taken down.

The surgeon exposes the pericardium after completing the level nine lymphadenectomy, revealing the posterior aspect of the inferior pulmonary vein. Using a robotic 30mm gray load tip-up stapler, the vein is encircled and divided. The procedure progresses to the posterior hilar dissection, focusing on the subcarinal space lymphadenectomy. The subcarinal space is visually defined by anatomical borders: the infrapulmonary vein as the base, the posterior aspect of the bronchus intermedius, the esophagus, and the carina as the apex, with the pericardium forming the floor.

The surgeon visualizes the triangle formed by the left and right main stem bronchus during the posterior hilar dissection. Using robotic instruments, the surgeon carefully removes the bulky level eleven interlobar lymph node in one piece, sacrificing the branch to the posterior segment of the upper lobe to achieve complete removal.

The surgeon completes the posterior hilar dissection and proceeds to the anterior hilar dissection, removing the level XI interlobar lymph node. The bronchus to the middle lobe and the bronchus to the lower lobe are visible. A delicate and complete hilar lymphadenectomy is performed, enhancing visualization of the pulmonary artery and improving the safety and oncologic efficacy of the procedure.

The surgeon removes the level XI lymph node, exposing the pulmonary artery and creating an entry point for a fissureless dissection technique. The pulmonary artery is accessed through anterior dissection, avoiding the need to navigate through the fissure. The lung parenchyma superficial to the pulmonary artery is divided, and the artery is then divided using a gray load stapler.

The surgeon exposes and divides the bronchus to the lower lobe using a green load stapler. The dissection continues with the removal of a bulky level eleven lymph node, carefully teasing it off from the bronchus to the upper lobe and pulmonary artery branches. A complete hilar and mediastinal lymphadenectomy is performed, followed by a level ten superior mediastinal lymphadenectomy and paratracheal lymph node dissection.

The surgeon defines the paratracheal space as a triangular area bordered by the trachea posteriorly, the superior vena cava anteriorly, and the pulmonary artery inferiorly, with the apex near the subclavian artery. The pericardium forms the floor of the dissection. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon removes level X lymph node, ensuring hemostasis and avoiding injury to the phrenic and vagus nerves. Pericardial fluid is suctioned from a small area where the pericardium is entered.

The surgical field shows a well-defined triangle with no residual lymphatic tissue remaining. The bedside assistant uses a suction device through the accessory port to maintain clear visualization, indicating a complete dissection.
